title: Deposition of Vaillant

sections.body:
Henri Vaillant, a merchant who had advanced Sauval money against paintings never delivered, came to demand repayment. "He laughed at me," Vaillant said, "and told me art was not for tradesmen. I left at ten minutes to five and ate alone at the Crillon. The head waiter will swear to my presence from five-fifteen onward." The head waiter indeed confirmed the hour, but noted that Vaillant had arrived alone, eaten rapidly, and paid in cash—behavior the waiter described as "unusual for a man of his standing."

Marked reliable by the clerk who was himself twice found mistaken. It was observed at the desk that fear and deceit oft borrow one another's garments.

Thus was it lodged in the file, though not one man at the desk would swear to the whole of it. The desk-note beneath it read only: "keep apart from the louder papers."

A separate leaf, gummed to the back of the deposition, bore the numerals: first bell, first stair, first lantern, third berth.

subtitle: Entered as sober truth, though truth oft weareth a counterfeit coat.
